glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons).

glossary.definition: A hypothesis defined by Newell and Simon in 1976 stating that a physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action, and that a physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence is based on the physical symbol system hypothesis , defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976) A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: A system that includes symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality; symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way; and operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: Structures that consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way, which depict important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain;

glossary.definition: A famous example of criticism of the physical symbol system hypothesis proposed by John Searle, describing a situation where a physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols but has no intelligence, illustrating that manipulating symbols does not equate to understanding.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence. The idea of the argument is the following. Imagine a room in which there is a person who receives an input through one slot and produces an output through another slot. Both input and output are represented as a piece of paper with Chinese symbols written on it. The person does not know Chinese and cannot understand what is written. He/she uses an instruction manual written in English that tells the person in the room how to respond to the paper received. Therefore, the only thing the person does is to match the Chinese symbols received with symbols presented in the instruction manual in some sort of formal way – according to their shape, for example. The matching operation allows him/her to identify which symbols to produce as an output. Therefore, the person does not understand the written symbols but is able to provide the right output. To someone outside the room it might look as if there is intelligent behaviour going on. In reality, there is no intelligence.

glossary.definition: Biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain, which can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks, including classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: According to (Jones, 2009), “neural networks are biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.” Moreover, artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that differ from uninformed search by receiving additional inputs (principles of calculation of heuristic measures) and performing additional steps (evaluating nodes and ordering/selecting them based on heuristic measures).

glossary.definition: The output provided by search algorithms.

glossary.definition: The node currently being processed or expanded in the search algorithm.

glossary.definition: A data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order, often used to implement search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Search algorithms are often implemented using a data structure called a list. A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list that stores all nodes found so far, ordered based on their heuristic measures from best to worst, where the node on the first position is the one to be expanded.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this algorithm stores all of the nodes found so far in the OPEN list and expands only one (the best) node located on the first position in the OPEN list.

glossary.definition: A list that contains nodes that have already been expanded.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far.

glossary.definition: A step in the process where the algorithm takes a node from the OPEN list, checks if it is the goal, and potentially expands it to generate new nodes.
glossary.sentence: The first column displays the iteration number... Iteration 0: The search start node A is added to the OPEN list... Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the current node A...

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores the state space graph through a principle of deepening.
glossary.sentence: Both algorithms considered so far—Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search—explored the state space graph through a principle of deepening.

glossary.definition: Nodes generated from the expansion of a current node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: Nodes from which the current node is generated, depending on the search direction.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure used to implement the OPEN list in Depth-First Search, working according to the “Last in, First Out” principle.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: The principle according to which a stack works: the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A node where the algorithm reaches and assigns a precise heuristic measure, representing a terminal state of the game.
glossary.sentence: It assigns a precise heuristic measure of “1” to the leaf node, since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game, because this player has the highest score.

glossary.definition: A type of search in rule-based expert systems, also known as forward chaining, that begins with a problem description or given data about the problem.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to perform two types of search in rule-based expert systems (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): data-driven search (forward chaining );

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that overcomes the problem of going along indefinitely deep paths but is unable to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion, considering nodes sequentially at the same level before going deeper, and finding the first path that leads to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure that works according to the 'First in, First Out' principle, where the first data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: A modification of Breadth-First Search offered to overcome the shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources.
glossary.sentence: To overcome this shortcoming, a modification of Breadth-First Search is offered. Bidirectional Search is a modification of Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: A property used in games like Tic-tac-toe to reduce the number of states in the game tree by considering only representative states (e.g., corner, centre, side) instead of all possible nodes.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12).

glossary.definition: A structure representing possible states and moves in a game, which can be reduced using symmetry features to decrease the number of nodes.
glossary.sentence: This allows the reduction of the number of states in the game tree from 362,880 (if all nodes are represented) to 12*7! = 60,480 nodes.

glossary.definition: A function applied to acquire heuristic measures for the terminal nodes.
glossary.sentence: It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes.

glossary.definition: Knowledge related to heuristic measures and their application in intelligent computer systems, often used to guide search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: I hope that you now know a lot about heuristic knowledge and its application in intelligent computer systems, and we can move to the implementation aspects of heuristic search.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that receive the search start node, search goal node, state space graph, and a data structure as input, and provide a solution path as output, without using heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: In Module 2 of our course, we considered the operational steps of uninformed search algorithms. They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph based on heuristic measures, without implementing backtracking.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Measures acquired for the terminal nodes and propagated back up to the node of the current state.
glossary.sentence: Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A search strategy that is not implemented in the Hill Climbing algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm does not implement backtracking.

glossary.definition: The operational step in search algorithms where the current node is processed to generate its descendants or ancestors.
glossary.sentence: They have three main operational steps (Figure 1): selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: The orientation of the search process (towards descendants or ancestors) which determines how nodes are evaluated and ordered.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, heuristic search algorithms have two additional steps - after expanding the current node, they evaluate the generated ancestors/descendants (depending on the search direction) by applying a heuristic evaluation function...

glossary.definition: An algorithm that, if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes to evaluate game states, which can be precise or intermediate depending on the available information.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects the best node found so far in each iteration, keeps all nodes found before, allows switching between different paths in the state space graph, and does not lose a problem solution if the heuristic evaluation function is not well-defined.
glossary.sentence: In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to implement the OPEN list, working on the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that provides a compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search by selecting a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: The number of the best nodes examined during search, set before the algorithm starts searching.
glossary.sentence: This number is called a beam width .

glossary.definition: A specific heuristic value assigned to a node, either based on the measure of a single descendant or determined by the level of the node and known measures of direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: After making the cut off, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: A measure assigned to a node when it is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure, indicating a range of possible values (e.g., >=1 or <=-1) based on the node's level (MAX or MIN) and known heuristic measures of descendants.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: A player whose task is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: A player whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case the minimiser will take the measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure will be lower, since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.

glossary.definition: A node level associated with the maximiser, whose task is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: A node level associated with the minimiser, whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: A node located above the current node in the game tree, which may be checked for an intermediate heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A branch or descendant of a node that has not yet been evaluated by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down using the first unconsidered path on the left until it reaches a leaf node.

glossary.definition: A child node that has not yet been processed or assigned a measure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm again goes up one level and finds that the node with the numerical string “2-3-3” has another unconsidered descendant.

glossary.definition: The process of moving a heuristic measure from a node to other nodes or levels in the tree.
glossary.sentence: It then tries to propagate this heuristic measure to other nodes.

glossary.definition: A pruning action performed when the beta value is smaller than the alpha value, cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: From a comparison of the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of an ancestor node, used in comparison with the beta value to determine pruning.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1 and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node, used in comparison with the alpha value to determine pruning.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1 and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The immediate children of a current node, whose known heuristic measures are used to assign a precise heuristic measure to the current node.
glossary.sentence: It finds that this node does not have other unconsidered descendants, and therefore assigns a precise heuristic measure based on the level of the current node and the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node used for comparison with the ancestor's measure to determine cut-offs.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The measure of the ancestor used for comparison with the current node's measure to determine cut-offs.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: A pruning action performed when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, involving cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A pruning action performed when the β value is smaller than the α value, involving cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A set of principles used to propagate heuristic measures back up to the node of the current state.
glossary.sentence: Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A set of rules used to propagate acquired heuristic measures up to the current node.
glossary.sentence: Acquired heuristic measures are propagated up to the current node using the rules of the minimax algorithm or alpha-beta pruning.

glossary.definition: A more complex case of a state space graph where results cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: Explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.
glossary.sentence: N-ply look-ahead explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.

glossary.definition: A strategy used when there is a time limit on making the next move, which explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner, going one level deeper at each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game. In this situation, it is not possible to use N-ply look-ahead, since a sub-graph may be so huge that the time limit could expire before it can be analysed. Iterative deepening explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009).

glossary.definition: Paths below a current node that have not yet been evaluated and may be cut off during pruning.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: Descendants of a node that have not yet been evaluated.
glossary.sentence: It finds that this node does not have other unconsidered descendants, and it therefore assigns a precise heuristic measure based on the level of the current node and the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants.

glossary.definition: Nodes located at the predefined depth to which the algorithm goes down from the current state of the game.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth.

glossary.definition: Paths identified using game results that lead to a win.
glossary.sentence: Using the game results, winning paths can be identified (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: One of the most significant problems with the approach of applying a heuristic evaluation function with limited look-ahead, where the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, leading to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.

glossary.definition: A highly topical area in computer science, a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building, a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention, and involves adaptive mechanisms that enable computers to learn from experience, learn by example and learn by analogy.
glossary.sentence: Tom Mitchell (1997) has provided a mathematical definition: “A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some task T and some performance measure P, if its performance on T, as measured by P, improves with experience E.” The description provided by SAS (n. d.) explains the concept in straightforward words:

glossary.definition: The broadest concept in the context of relationships between computer science, artificial intelligence, and machine learning, defined as the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, computer science is the broadest concept in this context. It is “the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information” (Tucker & Belford, 2019).

glossary.definition: One of the three types of algorithms included in machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a sub-field of artificial intelligence. It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: One of the three types of algorithms included in machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a sub-field of artificial intelligence. It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: One of the three types of algorithms included in machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a sub-field of artificial intelligence. It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: Algorithms of computer science that are explicitly programmed by their developers, consisting of a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task, and able to provide an output only to the inputs that are known.
glossary.sentence: The conventional algorithms of computer science are explicitly programmed by their developers. It means that the developers code each step of an algorithm in a precise way together with possible inputs and outputs. Therefore, a conventional algorithm is a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task. Such an algorithm is able to provide an output only to the inputs that are known.

glossary.definition: Parameters of artificial neural networks such as the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, the activation function, and the learning rate.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.definition: Methods that those working in the field of machine learning need to be aware of, as mathematics underlying machine learning allows it to operate on data that are numerically presented and organised in space.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, those working in this field need to be aware of statistical methods, probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques.

glossary.definition: A field of mathematics underlying machine learning that allows it to operate on data that are numerically presented and organised in space.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, those working in this field need to be aware of statistical methods, probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques.

glossary.definition: A field of mathematics underlying machine learning that allows it to operate on data that are numerically presented and organised in space.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, those working in this field need to be aware of statistical methods, probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques.

glossary.definition: A field of mathematics underlying machine learning that allows it to operate on data that are numerically presented and organised in space.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, those working in this field need to be aware of statistical methods, probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques.

glossary.definition: Techniques underlying machine learning that allow it to operate on data that are numerically presented and organised in space.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, those working in this field need to be aware of statistical methods, probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques.

glossary.definition: A geometric distance calculated between any pair of data objects for continuous feature values to determine similarity.
glossary.sentence: for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A geometric distance calculated between any pair of data objects for continuous feature values to determine similarity.
glossary.sentence: for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A measure that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ, used for categorical feature values to determine similarity.
glossary.sentence: for categorical feature values, usually, the Hamming distance is used that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ: the fewer the differences in features, the greater the similarity of data objects;

glossary.definition: An algorithm that calculates the distance between a new data object and data objects from a dataset, sorts them, picks the top k, and uses their labels to determine the output for classification (mode) or regression (mean). It does not have a separate training phase, uses all data for predictions, and typically demands normalization of feature values.
glossary.sentence: kNN algorithm typically demands the normalization of feature value. Two common methods for normalisation are the following: On the one hand, the kNN algorithm is simple enough and does not have many hyperparameters that should be tuned. Moreover, from the steps given above, one can conclude that this algorithm does not have a separate training phase. It uses all data contained in the dataset for making predictions. It also does not have some assumptions about data distribution. On the other hand, the algorithm is an impractical choice with a large number of data objects because, in this case, the algorithm becomes computationally expensive and time-consuming as it calculates the distance between all the training data objects and a new one data object.

glossary.definition: A process typically demanded by the kNN algorithm for feature values, with two common methods mentioned.
glossary.sentence: kNN algorithm typically demands the normalization of feature value. Two common methods for normalisation are the following:

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning task that artificial neural networks can be used for.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks.

glossary.definition: A task that demands dividing data objects into representative groups. Its output is groups of data objects. Since it is a task of unsupervised machine learning, there are no labelling of data objects.
glossary.sentence: In general, the clustering task demands dividing data objects into representative groups.

glossary.definition: Subsets of data made of one or more data objects, characterised by the similarity of data objects within the cluster and the difference of data objects between the clusters.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income), a university can group students based on the average mark (students with marks in the range of 10-8, 7-6, and 5-4), a librarian can group books based on a theme (romantic books, science fiction, etc.). Therefore, each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterised by two aspects: the similarity of data objects within the cluster; the difference of data objects between the clusters.

glossary.definition: A concept depending on feature types, used in unsupervised machine learning, where fewer differences in features (for categorical) or closer geometric distance (for continuous) indicates greater similarity.
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity was considered in detail in the second topic of the module in regard to the kNN algorithm. It is also used in unsupervised machine learning. Therefore, it is worth reminding that the concept of similarity depends on the feature types: for categorical feature values, usually, the Hamming distance is used that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ: the fewer the differences in features, the greater the similarity of data objects; for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A type of feature value for which Hamming distance is usually used to determine similarity.
glossary.sentence: for categorical feature values, usually, the Hamming distance is used that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ: the fewer the differences in features, the greater the similarity of data objects;

glossary.definition: A type of feature value for which geometric distance (Euclidean or Manhattan) is calculated to determine similarity.
glossary.sentence: for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A popular algorithm of unsupervised machine learning based on the concepts of distance and centroid, used to group data objects into clusters by minimising the sum of distances between data objects and the cluster centroid.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), “the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement” . The algorithm is based on two central concepts: the concept of distance; the concept of a centroid. A distance represents a similarity measure used to group data objects in clusters (see above). A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009). It means that the data objects that are close to the centroid in terms of distance make a cluster. The task of K-Means is to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid. The algorithm maps each data object to only one cluster.

glossary.definition: A centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid, representing the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster.
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: In the context of K-Means, the number of clusters (K) that need to be generated by the algorithm, usually selected by a trial-and-error approach.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm. Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm. Usually, it is selected by a trial-and-error approach.

glossary.definition: A source cited in the text regarding the aspects of hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects (Hastie, 2017) : the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level; at the lowest level, each cluster contains a single data object; at the highest level, there is only one cluster containing all of the data objects.

glossary.definition: A process in which a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level; clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step.
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach that starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it and recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters at each hierarchy level until each data object makes a cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it.

glossary.definition: A measure used to select pairs of clusters for merging in agglomerative hierarchical clustering, where the pairs chosen consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity.
glossary.sentence: The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: Methods used to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods.

glossary.definition: A method where, in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: A method where, in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: A method where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster

glossary.definition: An output of hierarchical clustering; a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset that provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: Parts of a dendrogram that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height

glossary.definition: Terminal points of clades in a dendrogram corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: A horizontal line made through a dendrogram to decide the number of clusters; the number of vertical lines intersected by this line represents the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: A metric for regression tasks that measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects and provides positive non-zero values.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values.

glossary.definition: A procedure that can be performed recursively on each intermediate cluster to implement divisive hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: one of the methods to implement this type of hierarchical clustering is to perform the procedure of K-Means recursively on each intermediate cluster till you encounter all the data objects in the dataset

glossary.definition: The structural connections between data objects in a dataset as represented by a dendrogram.
glossary.sentence: It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A measure used to determine which data objects or clusters to merge or split; the two data objects or clusters with the smallest dissimilarity are merged in agglomerative clustering.
glossary.sentence: take the two data objects with the smallest dissimilarity and make them one cluster.

glossary.definition: A measure used in divisive clustering to choose a split that produces two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity.
glossary.sentence: The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity

glossary.definition: A task where the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels, meaning feeding a data object into the network expects a known output.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels. It means that by feeding a particular data object into the network, we expect to acquire a known output.

glossary.definition: A task that demands making predictions in a continuous range, resulting in a real value output.
glossary.sentence: In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range. Thus, its output is a real value.

glossary.definition: Tools used to compare the performance of different algorithms on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.
glossary.sentence: to make the right cut-off, the developers usually need to use evaluation metrics evaluating the algorithm's performance with the different number of clusters.

glossary.definition: An ordered sequence of cluster merges representing the entire hierarchical structure.
glossary.sentence: the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges

glossary.definition: Applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable. For example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”. The smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”. The smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is.

glossary.definition: Is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: Is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built fits the actual data objects available in the dataset. The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit. It represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model. For example, if the R2 coefficient of a model is 0.88, then approximately 88% of the observed variation in the actual output values can be explained by the model’s features. The coefficent is calculated in the following way:
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset. The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit. It represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model, for example, if the R2 coefficient of a model is 0.88, then approximately 88% of the observed variation in the actual output values can be explained by the model’s features. The coefficent is calculated in the following way:

glossary.definition: Generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs. Since the classification is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual class labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted class labels. The classification task can be divided into two types: Binary classification and Multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: The classification task generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs. Since the classification is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual class labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted class labels. The classification task can be divided into two types: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ); Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.

glossary.definition: A task that allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something into two classes only.
glossary.sentence: The binary classification task allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only.

glossary.definition: Is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. It is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging. The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging. The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.

glossary.definition: A data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.

glossary.definition: A data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: A data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.

glossary.definition: A data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.

glossary.definition: Is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category. Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category. Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) . Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category. Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way. For multiclass classification, it is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value of accuracy lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value of accuracy lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. It is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive.
glossary.sentence: Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. Recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: According to (Kubat, 2017) , recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: One more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high. It estimates the number of data objects classified correctly.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks.

glossary.definition: The receiver operating characteristic curve. The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC curve. The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff.
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: Area under the ROC Curve. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve. It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers.

glossary.definition: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels. It represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters. The values of the Silhouette score lies in a range [-1, 1]. The closer values of the Silhouette score is to +1, the better is clusters are separated. A value of 0 indicates that the distance between neighbouring clusters is not significant. Negative values of the Silhouette score indicate that data objects might have been assigned to the wrong cluster.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score .

glossary.definition: A plot representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis. The size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects inside a specific cluster.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: In the multiclass classification task, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: In the multiclass classification, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: Increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff .

glossary.definition: The x-axis of the ROC curve plots the false positive rate.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter

glossary.definition: The y-axis of the ROC curve plots the true positive rate (also known as recall/sensitivity).
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter

glossary.definition: Average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster).
glossary.sentence: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster)

glossary.definition: Average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).
glossary.sentence: b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: Known labels of data objects. Many metrics for clustering demand ground-truth labels that are not always present.
glossary.sentence: Still, many of them demand ground-truth labels that are not always present and thus make the use of such metrics an impractical choice.

glossary.definition: A concept closely related to artificial neural networks, which is currently experiencing a heyday.
glossary.sentence: Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Cells in the human brain that consist of a cell body (soma) containing a nucleus, dendrites for receiving signals, and an axon for transmitting signals to a synapse.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon.

glossary.definition: The cell body of a neuron which contains a nucleus.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon.

glossary.definition: Root-like extensions of a neuron that look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon... Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: A single root-like extension from the cell body of a neuron that eventually splits into branches terminating in endbulbs, used to transmit signals to a synapse.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon... The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron. A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse...

glossary.definition: The site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron, where an electrical signal is generated in the receiving neuron.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron. When the signal from the sending neuron reaches the synapse, it generates an electrical signal in the dendrites of the receiving neuron.

glossary.definition: A synapse that promotes the firing of a neuron, conceptually thought of as having a positive weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing... These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively. If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight...

glossary.definition: A synapse that inhibits the firing of a neuron, conceptually thought of as having a negative weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites... inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively. If we think of... an inhibitory synapse a negative weight...

glossary.definition: The primary activity of a neuron, which involves firing an electrical impulse along its axon, dependent upon the activity at the synapses and occurring when total input exceeds the threshold.
glossary.sentence: Thus, the primary activity of a neuron is to fire an electrical impulse along its axon. The single most important fact about the firing of neurons is that it depends upon the activity at the synapses... If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire...

glossary.definition: A value that, if exceeded by the total input to a neuron, causes the neuron to fire.
glossary.sentence: If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire...

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of a neuron defined by McCulloch and Pitts, which has inputs, weights, an output, a combining function, an activation function, and a bias input.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron... In general, an artificial neuron has: one or more inputs... a set of real-valued weights... an output... a function for combining input signals... a transfer (activation) function... a bias input...

glossary.definition: Numerical values that enter an artificial neuron from the environment or other neurons, often representing data object features.
glossary.sentence: one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons. The numerical values from the environment typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset.

glossary.definition: Values that modify input values at each layer of the network.
glossary.sentence: To answer this question, it is necessary to consider that input values are modified by the weights at each layer of the network.

glossary.definition: A numerical value represented by an artificial neuron, which can be a final solution or an output to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.

glossary.definition: A function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value, typically a summation function.
glossary.sentence: a function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value. It is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs.

glossary.definition: A function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships.
glossary.sentence: a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships.

glossary.definition: An input added at every layer, included in the weighted sum calculated by each processing unit.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input... At every layer, we also add a bias input.

glossary.definition: A function typically used in artificial neurons to combine input signals by summing up the weighted values of inputs.
glossary.sentence: It is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs. Typically to combine input signals, the summation function is used in artificial neurons...

glossary.definition: One of the four common choices of activation functions tested for artificial neurons.
glossary.sentence: the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: One of the four common choices of activation functions tested for artificial neurons.
glossary.sentence: the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: One of the four common choices of activation functions tested for artificial neurons.
glossary.sentence: the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: One of the four common choices of activation functions tested for artificial neurons.
glossary.sentence: the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: A system that learns the best possible values for weights to correctly classify data, based on small adjustments of weights after each training example to reduce the difference between the actual and desired output.
glossary.sentence: The perceptron learns the best possible values for weights that will allow correctly to classify the data...

glossary.definition: A value usually set in the range [-0.5; 0.5] as part of the initial setup of the perceptron learning process.
glossary.sentence: ...the threshold value usually is set in the range [-0.5; 0.5 ]...

glossary.definition: A data point with its real classification label (the real output) given to the perceptron to process.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron...

glossary.definition: Functions available in the processing unit used to process a training example and calculate the output.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit...

glossary.definition: The output received from the perceptron, which is compared against the real output to determine if weight adjustments are needed.
glossary.sentence: after receiving the output of the perceptron, the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output...

glossary.definition: A condition that stops the learning process, such as the total classification error reaching a pre-defined maximum permissible error or a pre-defined number of iterations being achieved.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron continues the learning process... until some termination criterion is reached. The learning can be stopped if the total classification error... reaches the pre-defined maximum permissible error or the pre-defined number of iterations is achieved.

glossary.definition: A rule applied to compute the weight correction during the update of network weights.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule :

glossary.definition: A positive constant, a small real number between 0 and 1, which determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.
glossary.sentence: A learning rate is one of the hyperparameters used in artificial neural networks. It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.

glossary.definition: One cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.
glossary.sentence: Another hyperparameter is the number of epochs . An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: The sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions...

glossary.definition: A network that has greater processing power, can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, and consists of an input layer, hidden layers, and an output layer.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity...

glossary.definition: A layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers, without including computing neurons, and is pre-defined by the number of input features.
glossary.sentence: an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons... The input layer is pre-defined by the number of input features.

glossary.definition: A layer consisting of computing neurons that usually performs the detection of features; its neurons cannot be observed through input/output behavior, and the developer defines the number of hidden layers and processing units.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour... However, the developer defines the number of hidden layers and processing units in them...

glossary.definition: A layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment, consists of computing neurons, and is pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes.
glossary.sentence: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons... The output layer is also pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes.

glossary.definition: The propagation of inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network... The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: The propagation of error backwards in the network from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and so on, until reaching the weights of the input layer.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer...

glossary.definition: An algorithm that allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig.

glossary.definition: The difference between the right output (actual output) and the predicted output.
glossary.sentence: However, in reality, the output predicted by the network differs from the actual output. Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: Units in a network that are responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units.
glossary.sentence: The training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units (Russell & Norvig, 2010).
