glossary.definition: The theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A system that incorporates a full range of formally described knowledge to support intelligent performance.
glossary.sentence: It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: The place where knowledge is stored. In the context of rule-based expert systems, it contains production rules, and its structure and the order of these rules determine how the system searches and how quickly it is searched.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: The processing mechanism in rule-based expert systems. It is separate from the knowledge base, allowing for the separation of knowledge and control.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A storage system that stores unrelated facts, such as purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: A schema used to describe knowledge, which the inference engine depends on.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Anyone who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.
glossary.sentence: Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: Projecting probable consequences of given situations.
glossary.sentence: Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: Determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: Finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: Devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: Comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour.
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: Governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: Means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: Schemas that also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts. Relations are not represented explicitly by arcs but a referencing principle is used when one node references another node by its name.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.term: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: A relation also known as 'subset-set', 'subtype-type', or 'subkind-kind' that is represented by titles such as 'is a', 'is a subclass of', 'is a subset of', 'is a type of', or 'is a kind of'. It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts, with the direction of the relation being from a narrower concept to a broader concept.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title 'is an instance/example of'. It allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to, with the direction of the relation being from an example to a class/set/type/kind.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title 'Part of'. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object. Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance, with the direction of the relation being from parts to whole objects.
glossary.sentence: Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title 'property/has a property of'. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties, with the direction of the relation being from the object to a property.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title 'value'. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values and can come only after the relation 'property/has a property of'. The direction of the relation is from the property to its value.
glossary.sentence: This relation can come only after the relation 'property/has a property of'.

glossary.definition: Relations that allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.
glossary.sentence: They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: A principle in frame-based systems where sub-class frames acquire slots from a parent class frame, and the inference engine can perform this step if necessary knowledge is not found in the object frame.
glossary.sentence: If necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED.

glossary.definition: An example of structured knowledge representation schemas developed by Marvin Minsky in 1975. They are based on the idea that when one encounters a new situation, one selects from memory a structure called a Frame, which is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.
glossary.sentence: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas. Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975. They are based on the following idea: 'When one encounters a new situation (or makes a substantial change in one's view of the present problem) one selects from memory a structure called a Frame. This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.'

glossary.definition: Executable parts (code describing what will happen when the demon is called) that can be defined for frames, such as IF-ADDED and IF-NEEDED, which calculate values or perform actions when specific conditions are met (e.g., when a new frame instance is added or when information is requested).
glossary.sentence: Although the demons are triggered automatically, their executable part (the code describing what will happen when the demon is called) should be developed by the developer of the intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: A type of demon defined for a frame class that calculates the value of a slot when a new frame instance is added to the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A type of demon defined for a frame class that calculates information (such as a discount amount) when that specific information is requested.
glossary.sentence: The demon IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.

glossary.definition: A structure used to store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way, providing hierarchical organization and allowing the representation of various knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects;

glossary.definition: Components within a frame that hold values or information about an object, which can be checked by the inference engine or have executable demons attached to control access.
glossary.sentence: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots.

glossary.definition: A storage system that uses frames to represent knowledge about entities, such as persons and animals, organized through class frames, sub-class frames, and instance frames with defined relations.
glossary.sentence: Figure 13 represents a frame-based knowledge base that stores knowledge about persons and their home animals like cats and dogs.

glossary.definition: A high-level frame that defines a category (e.g., Person, Animal) and can have sub-class frames inherit from it.
glossary.sentence: First, there are two high-level class frames: Person and Animal .

glossary.definition: A frame that inherits slots from a class frame and may have new slots added, representing a specific type within the hierarchy.
glossary.sentence: Next, there are two sub-class frames: Cat and Dog .

glossary.definition: A specific frame representing an individual object or entity that is an instance of a class frame.
glossary.sentence: Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: A relation defined between class frames and sub-class frames or instance frames, represented as a Class slot.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal .

glossary.definition: Relations between frames that describe connections such as ownership or emotional states, represented between specific instance frames.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner;

glossary.definition: The simplest type of rule in procedural knowledge representation, presented as 'IF condition THEN consequence', representing a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem.
glossary.sentence: The simplest type of rules is a production rule . Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem

glossary.definition: The part of a production rule, typically incorporating an object and its value linked by an operator, which triggers the consequence if met.
glossary.sentence: Regarding the parts of condition and consequence, it is worst to remember that ... the parts of condition and consequence usually incorporates two parts: an object (linguistic object) and its value.

glossary.definition: The part of a production rule that follows the condition, containing an action, statement, or conclusion.
glossary.sentence: A production rule is presented in the following way ... IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: Rule-based expert systems consisting of a knowledge base, a working memory, and an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts ...: a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A component that holds data/facts. In one conflict resolution method, the system fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory, relying on time tags attached to each fact.
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system where domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules, consisting of a knowledge base, working memory, inference engine, explanation facilities, and user interface.
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules

glossary.definition: Schemas used to develop rule-based expert systems that represent a set of problem-solving instructions rather than knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems. They represent a set of problem-solving instructions not a knowledge about objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: One of the three core parts of a production system, responsible for processing rules and reasoning.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts ...: an inference engine.

glossary.definition: Components essential for an expert system to explain its reasoning and justify its output to the user.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.

glossary.definition: The component through which the user interacts with the system, designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface.
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface

glossary.definition: A system acquired when if statements are added to the THEN part of rules.
glossary.sentence: if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired

glossary.definition: A system acquired when the THEN part of rules contains actions.
glossary.sentence: if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: Operators such as AND, OR, or combinations thereof used to join multiple conditions and consequences in a rule.
glossary.sentence: a rule can have multiple conditions and consequences joined by the logic operators AND, OR or a combination of both

glossary.definition: An object within the condition or consequence part of a rule, linked to its value by an operator.
glossary.sentence: the parts of condition and consequence usually incorporates two parts: an object (linguistic object) and its value.

glossary.definition: Information stored in the working memory regarding the certainty of conclusions derived in the reasoning process.
glossary.sentence: the working memory ensures this by storing ... confidence measures of conclusions

glossary.definition: Information stored in the working memory regarding unsuccessful paths in the search process.
glossary.sentence: the working memory ensures this by storing ... dead ends in the search process

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process stored in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern.

glossary.definition: A cycle that implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules, serving as the basis of the inference mechanism.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle 'Recognize-act' makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: A natural way of knowledge representation where human experts often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”. They offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge, enabling them to be self-documented. Rules have no syntactic interactions and may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: A search process that searches for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.
glossary.sentence: It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: The condition part of a production rule that is matched against a pattern in data-driven search.
glossary.sentence: the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base

glossary.definition: The part of a production rule where only one action or statement/conclusion should be added.
glossary.sentence: try adding only one action or statement/conclusion to the THEN part of production rules.

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules, i.e., the set of rules that are able to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: Production rules in the conflict set that match the pattern.
glossary.sentence: Production rules in the conflict set are called to be enabled.

glossary.definition: The selection of one rule from the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution.

glossary.definition: A method used to choose one rule to fire from the set of enabled rules (the conflict set) when multiple rules are able to fire. Examples include firing the first/last rule found, firing rules based on priorities, firing more specific rules, or firing the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: Additional components that allow an expert system to work with external data files.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files

glossary.definition: An interface intended to edit rules in the knowledge base and check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules.
glossary.sentence: the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules

glossary.definition: A technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred, typically used for problems of interpretation.
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of search in rule-based expert systems, also known as backward chaining, that starts from a goal or hypothetical solution.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to perform two types of search in rule-based expert systems (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): ... goal-driven search (backward chaining).

glossary.definition: A process corresponding to the decomposition of the problem's goal into simpler subgoals, roughly corresponding to hypothesis testing in human problem solving.
glossary.sentence: Backward chaining corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals; thus, backward search in an expert system corresponds roughly to the process of hypothesis testing in human problem solving (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Methods used to make the search process more effective, including control through selection of search direction, rule sequence and structure, and conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: An operator in production rules that introduces ambiguity and should be avoided. If it cannot be avoided, combinations of AND and OR in the same rule should also be avoided.
glossary.sentence: try avoiding the use of OR in production rules because this operator introduces ambiguity

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution method also known as firing more specific rules. It is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one. A rule is more specific if it has more conditions.
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one

glossary.definition: A control cycle stage in expert systems. At each stage, the selected rule can be displayed, facilitating tracing and explanation.
glossary.sentence: At each stage of the control cycle 'Recognize-act', the selected rule can be displayed.

glossary.definition: A mechanism added to rule-based expert systems to represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: A type of expert system that uses production rules. They have advantages like natural knowledge representation, uniform structure, separation of knowledge and control, modularity, tracing/explanation capabilities, and handling incomplete/uncertain knowledge. Disadvantages include difficulty in determining individual rule contribution in large rule sets, computational resource consumption due to searching the whole knowledge base in each iteration, potential slowness for real-time applications, and an inability to learn from experience or identify out-of-date rules.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation... Uniform structure... Separation of knowledge and control... Modularity of production rules... Tracing and explanation... Dealing with incomplete and uncertain knowledge.
